div.h1: Cache Line Behavior: Driver Globals Layout (.bss) and False Sharing Analysis
div.p: x86-64 Linux · sizeof(unsigned long) = 8 · Cache line = 64 bytes Hot path: mydevice_read() touches offsets 0–23 → all in CL0 → single cache miss fetches all three

g.text: Cache Line 0 [0x000 – 0x03F] 64 bytes
Cache Line 1 [0x040 – 0x07F] 64 bytes — struct cdev (first half)
Cache Line 2 [0x080 – 0x0BF] 64 bytes — struct cdev (second half)
Cache Line 3 [0x0C0 – 0x0FF] 64 bytes — mydevice_mutex ★ ISOLATED
Cache Line 4 [0x100 – 0x13F] 64 bytes — read_queue + write_queue
Slab-allocated buffer (separate physical page)
MESI Cache Coherency Protocol — Inter-CPU Impact
Legend
0x00 │ kernel_buffer │ char * │ 8 B │ Pointer to kzalloc'd slab page
0x08 │ buffer_data_len│ size_t │ 8 B │ Valid bytes in FIFO [0, buffer_size]
0x10 │ buffer_size │ size_t │ 8 B │ Total capacity; set at init / RESIZE ioctl
0x18 │ open_count │ atomic_t│ 4 B │ Concurrent open fd count (LOCK XADD)
0x1C │ dev_num │ dev_t │ 4 B │ major:minor encoded as uint32
0x20 │ read_count │ atomic_t│ 4 B │ Total read() calls (LOCK XADD)
0x24 │ write_count │ atomic_t│ 4 B │ Total write() calls (LOCK XADD)
0x28 │ mydevice_class │ ptr │ 8 B │ struct class * → /sys/class/mydevice/
0x30 │ mydevice_device│ ptr │ 8 B │ struct device * → udev trigger
0x38 │ proc_entry │ ptr │ 8 B │ struct proc_dir_entry * /proc/mydevice

div.p: ⚡ Hot path spatial locality mydevice_read() and mydevice_write() access kernel_buffer + buffer_data_len + buffer_size in tight sequence → offsets 0x00–0x17 → all three arrive in a single 64-byte cache line fetch from L2/L3. Typical L1 hit after first access: ~4 cycles.

g.text: 0x40 │ my_cdev.kobj.name │ char * │ 8 B │ kobject name pointer
0x48 │ my_cdev.kobj.entry │ list │16 B │ kobject list linkage
0x58 │ my_cdev.kobj.kset │ ptr │ 8 B │ kobject set pointer
0x60 │ my_cdev.kobj.ktype │ ptr │ 8 B │ kobject type ops
0x68 │ my_cdev.kobj.sd │ ptr │ 8 B │ sysfs dirent
0x70 │ my_cdev.kobj.refcount│atomic_t│ 4 B │ kobject reference count
0x74 │ my_cdev.kobj (pad) │ — │12 B │ alignment padding
0x80 │ my_cdev.owner │ ptr │ 8 B │ THIS_MODULE pointer
0x88 │ my_cdev.ops │ ptr │ 8 B │ → &mydevice_fops (file_operations)
0x90 │ my_cdev.list │ list │16 B │ cdev list linkage in kernel registry
0xA0 │ my_cdev.dev │ dev_t │ 4 B │ first device number (major:minor)
0xA4 │ my_cdev.count │ uint │ 4 B │ number of consecutive minors (=1)
0xA8 │ (padding / reserved)│ — │24 B │ struct padding to cache-line align

div.p: 📖 Access pattern my_cdev.ops is fetched once at VFS dispatch time (cdev_add → inode->i_cdev). After the initial open(), the file_operations pointer is cached in filp->f_op; my_cdev itself is not on the hot path for read/write.

g.text: 0xC0 │ mutex.owner │atomic_long│ 8 B│ 0=unlocked; LSB flags; upper=task ptr
0xC8 │ mutex.wait_lock │spinlock_t │ 4 B│ protects wait_list; IRQ-disabled spin
0xCC │ (spinlock padding) │ — │ 4 B│ natural alignment
0xD0 │ mutex.wait_list.next│ ptr │ 8 B│ list_head: first waiter
0xD8 │ mutex.wait_list.prev│ ptr │ 8 B│ list_head: last waiter
0xE0 │ (padding to 64 B) │ — │32 B│ remainder of cache line unused

div.p: ✅ False-sharing isolation achieved mydevice_mutex occupies its own cache line [0xC0–0xFF]. CPU0 acquiring the mutex (writing mutex.owner) does not invalidate the cache line containing buffer_data_len [0x08] on CPU1. Without this separation, every lock/unlock would bounce CL0 between CPUs even when buffer_data_len itself hasn't changed → ~20–40 extra cycles per op.

g.text: 0x100│ read_queue.lock │spinlock_t │ 4 B│ IRQ-safe; protects read_queue.head
0x104│ (spinlock padding) │ — │ 4 B│ alignment
0x108│ read_queue.head.next│ ptr │ 8 B│ first sleeping reader task entry
0x110│ read_queue.head.prev│ ptr │ 8 B│ last sleeping reader task entry
0x118│ write_queue.lock │spinlock_t │ 4 B│ IRQ-safe; protects write_queue.head
0x11C│ (spinlock padding) │ — │ 4 B│ alignment
0x120│ write_queue.head.next│ptr │ 8 B│ first sleeping writer task entry
0x128│ write_queue.head.prev│ptr │ 8 B│ last sleeping writer task entry
0x130│ (unused / future) │ — │16 B│ remainder of cache line

div.p: ⏳ Wait queue access pattern Both queues share one cache line. wake_up_interruptible acquires queue.lock (spinlock), walks queue.head list, sets each waiter TASK_RUNNING. Both queues landing on one cache line means the second poll_wait() call in .poll is a L1 hit after the first.

g.text: 0x000│ [valid FIFO data] │ uint8[] │N B │ indices [0, buffer_data_len)
N │ [free space] │ uint8[] │M B │ indices [N, buffer_size), M=buf_size-N

div.p: kzalloc(buffer_size, GFP_KERNEL) → SLUB slab or page allocator (for sizes > 4096). Memory is physically contiguous within the slab object. The kernel_buffer pointer in CL0 (offset 0x00) points here. First access (cold): L3 or DRAM miss (~100–300 cycles). Subsequent accesses (warm): L1/L2 hit (~4–12 cycles).

p.strong: Scenario A — Concurrent writer + reader (no mutex = RACE)

ol.li: CPU0 (writer): holds CL0 in Modified state; updates buffer_data_len = 50
CPU1 (reader): holds CL0 in Shared state; reads stale buffer_data_len = 0
CPU0 stores → CPU1's CL0 copy is Invalidated (MESIF protocol)
CPU1 re-fetches CL0 from L3 → pays ~30–60 cycle coherence penalty
Result without mutex: CPU1 may read 0 then 50 or any torn intermediate → data corruption / infinite wait / wrong EOF

p.strong: Scenario B — With mutex (correct M4 implementation)

ol.li: CPU0 acquires mutex: cmpxchg(mutex.owner, 0, task_ptr) → bus lock on CL3 CL3 transitions M on CPU0; invalidated on all other CPUs
CPU0 writes buffer_data_len in CL0 → CL0 Modified on CPU0
CPU0: mutex_unlock → memory barrier (smp_mb) ensures CL0 write is globally visible before CL3 is released
CPU1 acquires mutex: fetches CL3 (was Invalidated) from L3 → pays 1 miss
CPU1 reads buffer_data_len from CL0: fetches updated CL0 from L3 → pays 1 miss
Correct: CPU1 is guaranteed to see CPU0's write; no torn reads Total coherence cost per lock/unlock pair: ~2 cache-line round-trips ≈ 60–120 cycles

div.p: Scenario C — False sharing (what WOULD happen if mutex shared CL0) If mydevice_mutex were placed at offset 0x20 (inside CL0): • Every mutex_lock / mutex_unlock by any CPU writes mutex.owner in CL0 • This invalidates CL0 on all other CPUs — including their cached copies of buffer_data_len and buffer_size • Even a CPU that only wants to READ buffer_data_len must re-fetch CL0 after every lock operation by any other CPU • At 4 concurrent readers + 4 writers: CL0 bounces between 8 CPUs continuously → ~8 × 60 = 480 extra cycles per round-trip just for false sharing M4's layout puts mutex in CL3 → false sharing eliminated

g.text: █ Orange: buffer state (hot path — read/write per syscall)
█ Blue: atomic counters (LOCK XADD — no mutex needed)
█ Purple: kernel infrastructure pointers (write-once at init)
█ Red: mutex fields (CL3 — isolated to prevent false sharing)
█ Green: wait queue entries (CL4 — both queues share one cache line)
░ Gray: padding / alignment / unused

text.tspan: (separate physical page, may be in different cache set)

g.text: offset 0x40: my_cdev begins
offset 0x80: my_cdev continued
offset 0xC0: mydevice_mutex (★ isolated cache line)
offset 0x100: wait queue heads
CL0: primary contention point for buffer state
CL3: mutex contention point (separate = no false sharing)
